perturbation.original_text: 3.13 Eutectix Warranty. Eutectix warrants that the Product (i) will conform to the Specifications, (ii) will be free from manufacturing defects (including all defects in workmanship and any defects in materials that were not specified by Liquidmetal or Customer), ) will be free and clear of all liens, encumbrances, charges, claims, or adverse interests of any kind, (iii) will comply with, in all stages of manufacture and distribution to Liquidmetal or Customer, the terms of Section 10. The foregoing representations and warranties shall survive delivery, inspection and payment and shall run in favor of Liquidmetal and its Affiliates, and their respective successors, assigns and customers, both direct and indirect.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.13 Eutectix Warranty. Eutectix warrants that the Product (i) will conform to the Specifications, (ii) will be free from manufacturing defects (including all defects in workmanship and any defects in materials that were not specified by Liquidmetal or Customer), ) will be free and clear of all liens, encumbrances, charges, claims, or adverse interests of any kind. The foregoing representations and warranties shall survive delivery, inspection and payment and shall run in favor of Liquidmetal.
perturbation.explanation: By removing '(iii) will comply with, in all stages of manufacture and distribution to Liquidmetal or Customer, the terms of Section 10' and 'and its Affiliates, and their respective successors, assigns and customers, both direct and indirect.', Eutectix is no longer required to comply with all applicable laws, including those concerning product safety, and the warranty no longer extends to Liquidmetal's customers. This significantly reduces Eutectix's liability and could expose Liquidmetal and its customers to legal risks associated with non-compliant products. By limiting the beneficiary of the warranty, the clause also limits the implied warranty of merchantability to parties directly transacting with Eutectix.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification limits the scope of the warranty, potentially excluding the implied warranty of merchantability for downstream customers, which is a legally implied right under the UCC. UCC 2-314 mandates that goods be merchantable when sold, ensuring they are fit for ordinary purposes. By restricting the warranty's reach, the contract may be interpreted as an attempt to disclaim or limit these implied warranties for Liquidmetal's customers, which may not be legally permissible without explicit and conspicuous disclaimers, violating the implied warranty of merchantability.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. (1) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (a) pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) in the case of fungible goods , are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) run, within the variations permitted by the agreement , of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) conform to the promise or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ) other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modification to the warranty clause removes the requirement for Eutectix to comply with Section 10 in all stages of manufacture and distribution and limits the warranty's reach to Liquidmetal only, excluding its affiliates, successors, assigns, and customers. This omission could lead to a contradiction with the implied warranty of merchantability, as explained by the LLM, particularly for downstream customers. UCC 2-314, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states that a warranty that goods shall be merchantable is implied if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind, unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316). By restricting the warranty to Liquidmetal, the contract potentially attempts to disclaim or limit these implied warranties for Liquidmetal's customers without proper exclusion or modification as required by the UCC, thus creating a legal contradiction under the Omission Legal Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: 12.3 Insurance. Eutectix shall obtain, pay for, and maintain insurance meeting or exceeding the minimum insurance requirements set forth on Schedule 2 attached hereto, with policy terms satisfactory to Liquidmetal.
perturbation.changed_text: 12.3 Insurance. Eutectix shall obtain and maintain insurance as it deems necessary.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement to meet minimum insurance standards set forth on Schedule 2 exposes Liquidmetal to significant financial risk. The original clause ensured that Eutectix maintained adequate coverage for potential liabilities arising from the agreement. By omitting this, Eutectix could maintain minimal coverage, insufficient to cover potential claims. The contract now contradicts standard business practice and legal expectations regarding adequate insurance coverage for commercial operations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of contract and negligence laws requiring reasonable business practices
perturbation.law_citation: General contract and tort law principles
perturbation.law_explanation: By failing to specify minimum insurance requirements, the modified clause creates a legal contradiction with the implied duty of reasonable care in commercial contracts. Standard negligence laws require businesses to take reasonable precautions to prevent foreseeable harm to others. Adequate insurance coverage is a key component of reasonable risk management. Removing specific requirements makes it more difficult for Liquidmetal to prove that Eutectix breached its duty of care should an incident occur, shifting more risk onto Liquidmetal.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: negligence | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you negligence Negligence is the failure to behave with the level of care that a reasonable person would have exercised under the same circumstances. Either a person’s actions or omissions of actions can be found negligent. The omission of actions is considered negligent only when the person had a duty to act (e.g., a duty to help someone because of one’s own previous conduct). Negligence is a foundational concept of tort law. Some primary factors to consider in ascertaining whether a person’s conduct lacks reasonable care are the foreseeable likelihood that the conduct would result in harm, the foreseeable severity of the harm, and the burden of precautions necessary to eliminate or reduce the risk of harm. The following five elements may typically be required to prove negligence: The existence of a legal duty that the defendant owed the plaintiff Defendant’s breach of that duty Harm to the plaintiff Defendant’s actions are the proximate cause of harm to the plaintiff Defendant’s actions are the cause-in-fact of harm to the plaintiff Legal Duty of Care Generally, members of society have entered a social contract that includes a duty to not cause harm to others. When considering legal duty as an element of negligence, there is a duty to act reasonably. When a jury is asked to determine if a defendant’s actions were reasonable, the standard is what a reasonable person would have done in the defendant’s situation. The following is a non-exhaustive list of ways to determine if the defendant owed a duty of care to the plaintiff: The defendant engaged in the creation of risk which resulted in the plaintiff’s harm Voluntary undertaking: the defendant volunteered to protect the plaintiff from harm, possibly preventing others from protecting the plaintiff Knowledge: the defendant knows or should know that their conduct will harm the plaintiff Business/voluntary relationships: certain relationships—such as business owner and customer, innkeeper and guest, landowner and lessee, doctor and patient—create duties of care Breach of Duty of Care Some courts in the United States use the Hand Formula (created by Judge Learned Hand in United States v. Carroll Towing ) to determine if there was a breach of the duty of care to the plaintiff. If B < PL, then the defendant breached the duty of care. B=burden of taking precautions P=probability of loss L=severity of loss (personal loss, not societal loss) If the burden of taking such precautions is less than the probability of injury multiplied by the severity of the resulting injury, then the defending party breached their duty of care to the plaintiff and may be liable for the plaintiff’s injuries, if the remaining elements of negligence can be proven. This formula considers economic costs to the defendant in its approach to assessing liability. The idea is not to eliminate the risk of harm entirely, for complete elimination of risk would result in a high economical burden for the defendant that would then trickle down to costs for the consumer. Instead, the defendant is encouraged to take precautions that reduce either the probability of harm or severity of possible harm, such that their burden is less than the probability of harm multiplied by the severity of potential harm. Harm to the Plaintiff Typically, harm to the plaintiff must be either bodily harm or harm to property ( personal property or real property ). Harm that is solely economic usually will not satisfy this element in proving negligence. Some states recognize emotional distress as harm to the plaintiff, even if the harm is purely mental and not physical. Proximate Cause To satisfy proximate cause, the defendant’s breach of the duty of care must be the actual cause of the harm to the plaintiff. One way to assess proximate cause is to evaluate the foreseeability of the defendant’s actions resulting in harm. For example, if the d
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: CACI No. 300. Breach of Contract - Introduction :: California Civil Jury Instructions (CACI) (2025) :: Justia CACI No. 300. Breach of Contract - Introduction Judicial Council of California Civil Jury Instructions (2025 edition) Download PDF 300 . Breach of Contract - Introduction [ Name of plaintiff ] claims that [he/she/ nonbinary pr onoun /it] and [ name of defendant ] entered into a contract for [ insert brief summary of alleged contract ]. [ Name of plaintiff ] claims that [ name of defendant ] breached this contract by [ briefly state the alleged br each ]. [ Name of plaintiff ] also claims that [ name of defendant ]’ s breach of this contract caused harm to [ name of plaintiff ] for which [ name of defendant ] should pay . [ Name of defendant ] denies [ insert denial of any of the above claims ]. [ Name of defendant ] also claims [ insert affırmative defense ]. New September 2003; Revised December 2007 Directions for Use This instruction is designed to introduce the jury to the issues involved in the case. It should be read before the instructions on the substantive law . Sources and Authority • The Supreme Court has observed that “[c]ontract and tort are dif ferent branches of law . Contract law exists to enforce legally binding agreements between parties; tort law is designed to vindicate social policy .” ( Applied Equipment Corp. v . Litton Saudi Arabia, Ltd. (1994) 7 Cal.4th 503, 514 [28 Cal.Rptr .2d 475, 869 P .2d 454].) • “The dif ferences between contract and tort give rise to distinctions in assessing damages and in evaluating underlying motives for particular courses of conduct. Contract damages seek to approximate the agreed-upon performance . . . and are generally limited to those within the contemplation of the parties when the contract was entered into or at least reasonably foreseeable by them at that time; consequential damages beyond the expectations of the parties are not recoverable.” ( Applied Equipment Corp., supra, 7 Cal.4th at p. 515, internal citations omitted.) • Certain defenses are decided as questions of law , not as questions of fact. These defenses include frustration of purpose, impossibility , and impracticability . ( Oosten v . Hay Haulers Dairy Employees and Helpers Union (1955) 45 Cal.2d 784, 788 [291 P .2d 17]; Mitchell v . Ceazan T ires, Ltd. (1944) 25 Cal.2d 45, 48 [153 P .2d 53]; Autry v . Republic Pr oductions, Inc. (1947) 30 Cal.2d 144, 157 [180 P .2d 888]; Glen Falls Indemnity Co. v . Perscallo (1950) 96 Cal.App.2d 799, 802 [216 P .2d 567].) • “Defendant contends that frustration is a question of fact resolved in its favor by 83 the trial court. The excuse of frustration, however , like that of impossibility , is a conclusion of law drawn by the court from the facts of a given case . . . .” ( Mitchell, supra, 25 Cal.2d at p. 48, italics added.) • Estoppel is a “nonjury fact question to be determined by the trial court in accordance with applicable law .” ( DRG/Beverly Hills, Ltd. v . Chopstix Dim Sum Cafe and T akeout III, Ltd. (1994) 30 Cal.App.4th 54, 61 [35 Cal.Rptr .2d 515].) • “A settlement agreement is a contract, and the legal principles which apply to contracts generally apply to settlement contracts.” ( Monster Ener gy Co. v . Schechter (2019) 7 Cal.5th 781, 789 [249 Cal.Rptr .3d 295, 444 P .3d 97].) Secondary Sources 1 W itkin, Summary of California Law (1 1th ed. 2017) Contracts, §§ 872-892 13 California Forms of Pleading and Practice, Ch. 140, Contracts , § 140.50 (Matthew Bender) 5 California Points and Authorities, Ch. 50, Contracts , § 50.10 et seq. (Matthew Bender) 1 Matthew Bender Practice Guide: California Contract Litigation, Ch. 13, Attacking or Defending Existence of Contract - Absence of Essential Element , 13.03-13.17 CACI No. 300 CONTRACTS 84 © Judicial Council of California . perturbation.justification: The changed text omits the requirement for Eutectix to maintain insurance meeting minimum standards specified in Schedule 2. This omission creates a legal contradiction because it undermines the duty of reasonable care. Snippet 1 states that negligence is the failure to behave with the level of care that a reasonable person would under the same circumstances. The omission of actions is considered negligent only when the person had a duty to act. Removing the specified minimum insurance requirements allows for insufficient coverage, which increases the risk to Liquidmetal and could be seen as a breach of duty of care. Snippet 1 also notes 'the foreseeable likelihood that the conduct would result in harm, the foreseeable severity of the harm, and the burden of precautions necessary to eliminate or reduce the risk of harm'. By removing insurance, the likelihood of harm increases because the defendant is not insured for the harm.

perturbation.original_text: 3.14 Return Process. Liquidmetal and Eutectix shall concur in advance on all Liquidmetal Products to be returned for repair or rework. All returns shall state the specific reason for such return, and will be processed in accordance with the return policies and processes agreed to in writing by Eutectix and Liquidmetal. Eutectix shall pay all transportation costs for valid returns of Liquidmetal Products to Eutectix and for the shipment of repaired or replacement Liquidmetal Products to Customers, and Eutectix shall bear all risk of loss or damage to such Liquidmetal Products while in transit.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.14 Return Process. Liquidmetal and Eutectix shall concur in advance on all Liquidmetal Products to be returned for repair or rework. All returns shall state the specific reason for such return, and will be processed in accordance with return policies. Liquidmetal shall pay all transportation costs for returns of Liquidmetal Products to Eutectix and for the shipment of repaired or replacement Liquidmetal Products to Customers, and Liquidmetal shall bear all risk of loss or damage to such Liquidmetal Products while in transit.
perturbation.explanation: This modification places the burden of return shipping costs and risk of loss during transit on Liquidmetal, regardless of whether the return is due to Eutectix's fault (e.g., manufacturing defects). This directly contradicts consumer protection laws in many jurisdictions, including Arizona, which generally hold the seller responsible for return shipping costs for defective goods within a reasonable timeframe. By omitting the clause regarding 'valid returns' and shifting the financial responsibility entirely to Liquidmetal, the contract violates these consumer protection principles.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Arizona Consumer Protection Laws regarding defective goods
perturbation.law_explanation: Arizona law implies warranties of merchantability and fitness for a particular purpose, and consumers are generally entitled to remedies like repair, replacement, or refund for defective goods. Shifting return shipping costs and risk of loss to Liquidmetal, even when the product is defective due to Eutectix's actions, effectively limits these remedies and violates the spirit of Arizona's consumer protection laws. While Liquidmetal isn't a consumer in this specific contract, the clause impacts the cost for consumers who purchase defective Liquidmetal Products.

perturbation.justification: The modification removes the 'valid returns' condition and shifts the cost and risk of loss during transit to Liquidmetal regardless of the reason for the return. The Law Explanation states that this change limits remedies for defective goods, impacting consumers who purchase defective Liquidmetal products. Although Scraped Law Snippet 2 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines deceptive practices. The modified contract clause shifts financial responsibility to Liquidmetal, contradicting the consumer protection principles. The original text included provisions addressing 'valid returns' while the changed text omits this condition and alters responsibility for shipping costs, thus creating a legal loophole as described in the Omission Legal Contradiction definition.
